expectations.text: Questions are clearly delineated with one concept per question, not walls of text
expectations.evidence: Every question in the transcript is a single focused question under a bold **QUESTION:** label. Each covers one concept: Round 1 asks about the triggering moment, then separately about what happens if they don't build it. Round 2 asks about a specific person who benefits, then separately about what causes unsubscribes. No question contains multiple unrelated sub-questions or paragraph-length preambles.

expectations.text: At least 2 questions surface topics the initial prompt did not mention
expectations.evidence: The initial prompt was 'I want to start a weekly newsletter about AI tools for non-technical managers.' Topics surfaced that were not mentioned: (1) Liability/risk of recommending tools -- 'a reader emails you: I tried the tool you recommended and it gave my team completely wrong outputs that we sent to a client' (Round 7 Edge Finder). (2) Sustainability/burnout -- 'what's your backup plan for weeks when work gets intense?' (Round 5). (3) Monetization/business model (Round 6). (4) Distribution platform choice -- Beehiiv vs Substack (Round 4). At least 4 topics were surfaced beyond the initial prompt.

expectations.text: Later questions reference or build on earlier answers
expectations.evidence: Round 2 opens with 'Think about the 15 people you've been emailing' -- directly referencing the answer from Round 1 about sending informal emails to 15 people. Round 3 asks 'Walk me through what one issue contains' after learning the audience persona. The Simplifier Challenge says 'If you had to launch in 48 hours with issue #1, and it could only be one thing -- no secondary sections, no links roundup, just the core' which references the multi-section format described in Round 3. The progress check explicitly synthesizes prior answers before continuing.

expectations.text: At least 1 question challenges an assumption or pushes back on the idea
expectations.evidence: Two clear challenges: (1) Round 1: 'What happens if you don't build this?' -- directly challenges whether this needs to exist at all. (2) The Edge Finder Challenge in Phase 4: 'Six months in, a reader emails you: I tried the tool you recommended and it gave my team completely wrong outputs that we sent to a client. What do you do?' -- challenges the assumption that recommending tools is low-risk. The Simplifier Challenge also pushes back on scope by forcing the user to strip to the bare minimum.

expectations.text: The interview explicitly addresses what is NOT in scope
expectations.evidence: In the transcript, Round 3 asks 'Name two things readers will expect this newsletter to do that it won't.' The answer explicitly excludes comprehensive tool comparison roundups and enterprise AI strategy. The spec has a full 'Out of Scope' section listing 6 items: roundups, enterprise strategy, technical deep dives, paid tier, other media formats, and affiliate/sponsored content.

expectations.text: Final spec has clear sections including problem, scope, and success criteria
expectations.evidence: The spec (spec.md) has these explicit sections: '## Problem' (paragraph on the gap for non-technical managers), '## Scope' (with '### In Scope' and '### Out of Scope' subsections), and '## Success Criteria' (with quantified 6-month 'working' and 'not working' checkpoints). Also includes Goal, Audience, Design, Constraints, and Risks sections.

expectations.text: Key decisions are documented with rationale, not just listed
expectations.evidence: The spec's '### Key Decisions' section documents 5 decisions, each with explicit rationale. For example: 'Beehiiv over Substack: Better growth tools (referral program, recommendation network), full email list ownership. Substack has more organic discovery but locks you in.' And: 'One tool per issue, not roundups: Depth beats breadth. A single actionable walkthrough is worth more than five shallow mentions. This is the core differentiator.' Every decision includes the 'why,' not just the 'what.'

expectations.text: Spec identifies risks or open questions that remain unresolved
expectations.evidence: The spec has both a '### Open Questions' section (3 items: LinkedIn content strategy, liability framing, growth beyond LinkedIn -- each described as unresolved with proposed but not finalized approaches) and a '## Risks' table with 5 rows covering burnout, crowded market, stale recommendations, distribution plateau, and reader misuse -- each with likelihood, impact, and mitigation. The open questions explicitly use language like 'Not solved yet' and 'Defer this decision until month 3-4.'

claims.claim: The interview followed a structured 5-phase process (Intake, Investigation, Interview Loop, Challenge, Crystallize)
claims.evidence: Transcript clearly labels Phase 1 through Phase 5, with Phase 2 (Investigation) explicitly noted as skipped for a greenfield non-technical project.

claims.claim: 7 rounds of questions were conducted in the interview loop
claims.evidence: Transcript shows Round 1 through Round 7, each with 2 questions and answers, totaling 14 Q&A pairs in the main loop.
Scores listed: Intent 95, Outcome 80, Scope 85, Constraints 75, Audience 90, Criteria 80. Average = (95+80+85+75+90+80)/6 = 84.2%, which rounds to ~85%.

claims.claim: The spec covers a newsletter on Beehiiv targeting non-technical managers
claims.evidence: Spec explicitly states 'Platform: Beehiiv' under Constraints and audience is described as 'Mid-level managers (directors, senior managers, VPs) at mid-market companies.'

claims.claim: Success criteria include 2,000+ subscribers and 45%+ open rate at 6 months
claims.evidence: Spec section '## Success Criteria' lists '2,000+ subscribers' and '45%+ open rate' under the 6-month working checkpoint.

suggestions.assertion: At least 2 questions surface topics the initial prompt did not mention
suggestions.reason: This is easy to pass -- almost any interviewer will ask about something beyond the initial prompt. A more discriminating version would check that the surfaced topics led to substantive spec content, not just that they were raised.

suggestions.assertion: Later questions reference or build on earlier answers
suggestions.reason: The bar here is low: a single reference qualifies. A stronger assertion would require that at least 3 rounds demonstrate chaining, or that the interview would not make sense if rounds were reordered.

No assertion checks the quality of the answers -- the transcript has both questions and answers, and the answers feel realistic and internally consistent, but nothing validates whether the skill actually used the AskUserQuestion tool or simulated the conversation. If both sides were generated by the same model, the entire interview could be fabricated. Worth considering an assertion that checks the transcript for evidence of tool invocations.

No assertion checks whether the spec is traceable back to the interview -- that specific answers led to specific spec decisions. The spec could theoretically be a generic template that happens to match the topic. An assertion like 'the spec references at least 2 specific details from interview answers (e.g., Sarah, the 15 internal readers, 3-4 hours/week)' would catch this.

eval_feedback.overall: All 8 assertions pass genuinely -- the transcript and spec are both high quality. But the assertions skew toward structural checks (does the spec have sections? do questions cover scope?) rather than content quality. The riskiest gap is the lack of any check that the interview was actually interactive vs. fully generated in one pass.
